title: Modern Wood Storage Sofa
summary: A full-length 2x4-framed sofa with four hidden drawers and a pipe-rail back. Big, boxy, and heavy — but every board is a straight cut.
description:
This is a large piece built the honest way: 2x4 frames clad in 1x boards, with four drawers hidden under the seat riding on standard 26" drawer slides. There's no joinery to speak of — it's all self-tapping screws, glue, and brad nails — but the scale is what makes it a real project. The seat frames run 102-1/2" long, so you're working with 9-foot studs and you need floor space to assemble.

The one thing you cannot fudge: the two seat frames that carry the drawers must match each other exactly. Drawer slides are unforgiving of a frame that's 1/8" out of parallel — the drawer binds or won't close flush. Build both frames on the same layout marks, check them against each other before you clad anything, and shim the slides with scrap if an opening ends up a hair wide (that's what step 13 is for).

The pipe rail across the back is decorative black iron pipe — flanges, elbows, and tees — assembled and screwed on last. Buy the fittings pre-cut to length where you can; cutting and threading pipe yourself is a separate skill and a separate set of tools. Upholster the seat with a cushion sized to the finished opening.
timeLabel: 15–25 hrs — a full weekend or two, plus finishing

tools.note: A lot of repetitive square crosscuts. A stop block saves hours here.
For breaking down the long 9-ft stock and the plywood.
Optional — the frames can be built with self-tapping screws instead of pocket screws.
For the 1x cladding and trim. A hammer and finish nails work but will take much longer.
Predrill the drawer-face screws so the 1x doesn't split.

materials.name: 2x4, 9 ft (or 9 ft stud)
materials.note: The 102-1/2" seat and back rails come out of these.

materials.name: 1x8, 8 ft
materials.note: Arm cladding and the angled back boards.

materials.name: 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Cladding, drawer boxes, and drawer faces.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/4 in, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Drawer bottoms and the back panel (if the back is against a wall).

materials.name: Drawer slides, 26 in
materials.note: Full-extension side-mount. Buy all four the same brand and model.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, 2-1/2 in
materials.note: SPAX or similar — for the 2x4 frames.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, 2 in
materials.note: For attaching frames to arms and back.

materials.name: Black iron pipe fittings and nipples, 1/2 in
materials.note: 7 flanges, 5 elbows, 2 tees, plus the cut nipples listed in the plan. Buy pre-cut/threaded lengths.

materials.name: Seat cushion
materials.note: Sized to the finished seat. Foam + fabric, or a bought bench cushion.

cutList.note: Plus 4 x 1x6 @ 24" to fill — mix as needed to cover the arm ends.
Splice over the center stud; two pieces 24" x 51-1/4".
Both ends cut parallel at 15° off square, long-point to short-point.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four arm frame verticals at 24", four arm frame long members at 31-1/2", ten seat frame cross members at 9", four seat frame long rails at 102-1/2", two back frame long rails at 102-1/2" and three back frame studs at 21" from the 2x4. Cut 16 arm end cladding at 24" and 14 angled back boards at 12-3/4" from the 1x8. Cut four arm cladding 1x6 at 24", two arm top 1x6 at 36", one front trim at 102", eight drawer sides at 23-3/4" and eight drawer fronts/backs at 26" from the 1x6. For the back panel, follow the cut-list note: cut two 24" x 51-1/4" pieces from the 1/4 in plywood and splice them over the center stud, rather than trying to cut one 102-1/2" panel. Cut one back retaining strip at 102-1/2" from the 1x2. Cut two top trim at 102-1/2" from the 1x3. Cut four drawer bottoms at 26" from the 1/4 in plywood. Cut two drawer faces (wide) at 25-5/8" and two drawer faces (narrow) at 25-1/8" from the 1x10. Check your lumber before cutting: the longest 2x4 piece is 102-1/2", which will not come off the 8 ft length this plan lists, the longest 1x6 piece is 102", which will not come off the 8 ft length this plan lists, and the longest 1x3 piece is 102-1/2", which will not come off the 8 ft length this plan lists. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Frame and clad the two arms
steps.body:
Build two rectangular 2x4 frames for the arms with the 2-1/2" self-tapping screws. Check each for square before the screws go tight — an out-of-square arm throws the whole sofa off.

Clad the outside faces with 1x8 and 1x6 boards using glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, then add the 1x6 trim to finish the edges. Set both arms aside.

steps.title: Build the two seat frames — make them identical
steps.body: Build the two seat frames from the 102-1/2" rails and the 9" cross members, using the 2-1/2" screws. These frames carry the drawers, so they must match each other exactly. Lay them side by side and confirm the cross-member spacing lines up frame to frame before you move on. A frame that's out of parallel means a drawer that binds later.

steps.title: Build and panel the back frame
steps.body:
Build the back frame from the two 102-1/2" rails and three 21" studs, checking for square and using glue with the 2-1/2" screws.

If the sofa backs against a wall, splice the 1/4" plywood over the center stud (two pieces, 24" x 51-1/4") and attach with glue and 1-1/4" brads. If the back will be exposed to the room, leave it open for now — you'll clad it with 1x boards instead in the next step.

steps.title: Join the back to the arms
steps.body: Stand the arms up and attach the back frame to them with the 2" screws, driving from the inside where the heads won't show. If the back is exposed, add the extra 1x cladding boards now with glue and 1-1/4" brads. Check that the whole carcass sits flat and square before the drawers go in.

steps.title: Install the angled back boards and trim
steps.body:
Set the 15°-mitered 1x8 back boards inside the back opening and nail them down. Run the 1x2 retaining strip across the base of the back boards with glue and brads to lock them in place.

Trim the front and top edges of the whole sofa with 1x3 and 1x6 using glue and 1-1/4" brads. Fill nail holes and sand everything smooth before finishing.

steps.title: Build the four drawers and hang them on slides
steps.body:
Build four drawer boxes from 1x6 sides and fronts/backs with 1/4" plywood bottoms, sized to fit the openings. Mount the 26" slides so each drawer front sits flush with the front edge of the 2x4 frame. If a slide won't reach because the opening is a touch wide, block inward with scrap wood — that's normal and expected on a rough-framed carcass.

Attach the 1x10 drawer faces from inside with screws and glue, predrilling so the face doesn't split. Adjust each face for an even gap before the screws go tight.

steps.title: Add seat slats, the pipe rail, and the cushion
steps.body:
Lay scrap 1x boards (about 23" long) across the seat frame as slats and nail them down — they carry the cushion, so they don't need to be pretty.

Assemble the black iron pipe rail from the flanges, elbows, and tees, and screw the flanges to the back to hold it. Finish the wood, then drop in a cushion sized to the finished seat opening.
materials: Black iron pipe fittings and nipples, 1/2 in

images.alt: Modern wood sofa with hidden storage drawers and a black pipe back rail
